1: Psalm 123 is a communal psalm of petition during a period of social contempt and foreign oppression. The opening posture — eyes lifted to the enthroned God — is the prayer-posture of the expectant servant: "To you I lift my eyes (elecha nasati et-einai), O you who sit enthroned (hayoshevi) in the heavens!" The divine enthronement in heaven is the spatial claim that grounds all petition: YHWH is at the highest possible point, from which he sees all and from which no human power can dislodge him. To lift eyes to the enthroned one is to direct attention away from the circumstances of contempt (vv. 3-4) and toward the one who governs from above those circumstances. Ps 11:4; 113:5-6 use the same enthroned-heavens imagery of the God who "looks down" on human affairs from his elevated position.
2: "As the eyes of a servant watch the hand of his master, as a maidservant's eyes follow the hand of her mistress, so our eyes watch the LORD our God" — the ANE household communication protocol that supplies the psalm's central image. In a high-status household, verbal commands were often replaced by silent hand-signals: a gesture of the master's hand directed the servant to the next task, summoned them, or dismissed them. The servant who watched the hand most carefully was the most responsive and the most trusted — the one promoted to greater responsibility. The prayer-posture of watching the divine hand is thus not passive subjection but attentive readiness: the covenant community watches for the divine signal, prepared to respond immediately to whatever YHWH indicates. Both male (eved) and female (shifhah) servants are named, making the image comprehensive: all of the covenant community — not just one gender or class — holds this position of attentive watchfulness before YHWH.
3: "Have mercy on us, O LORD, have mercy on us (honenu YHWH honenu) — for we have endured far more than our fill of contempt (buz)" — the doubled imperative conveys both urgency and exhaustion: the community has waited so long that the standard petition must be intensified. Buz (contempt, scorn, the disregard of the powerful for the powerless) is one of the most painful social wounds in the honor-shame world — to be held in contempt was to be publicly designated as without honor, beneath notice, unworthy of serious engagement. This is not anger (which at least acknowledges the other person's significance) but dismissal: the contemptuous person does not bother to hate — they simply disregard. The community has been "glutted" with contempt (rabbat savenah-lah nafshenu, "our soul is fully sated — overfull") — they have consumed what should never have been on their plate at all.
4: "We have had more than our fill of the mockery of the comfortable (shaalvei) and the contempt of the arrogant (ge'im)" — the social profile of those whose contempt the community endures: shaalvei (those at ease, prosperous, undisturbed, comfortable) and ge'im (the arrogant, the haughty). The comfortable have been insulated from suffering by their prosperity; their contempt flows from having never been desperate or powerless. The arrogant have elevated themselves above their proper station and regard the covenant community from that false elevation. The psalm's petition to the enthroned God (v1) is motivated by this social structure: those who despise the community occupy positions of earthly height; the only height from which they can be overturned is the height of divine enthronement — where YHWH sits above them all.
